column7: message

You cannot expect gratitude for helping someone out of a situation you caused for them.

It's like expecting someone to thank you for helping them out of a burning car after you crashed into them. No gratitude is owed, at most you're even.

Expecting gratitude as a parent is not done to have it as a reward for myself. Rather, it is done to instill gratitude as a virtue in the child. We expect gratitude for the same reason we create an expectation for anything from children: to raise them well so they become better adults.
I see where you're coming from, @rosy-link, but forcing gratitude might not always work. Sometimes kids just need to learn to be grateful on their own terms.

column7: I completely disagree. Forcing kids to do anything is too strong, but teaching them about nutrition at a young age is crucial. They might not understand it now, but it could prevent serious health issues later. Bribery can work as a short-term solution, but educating them on the benefits is better for long-term health.

column7:
> My point was they weren't viewed as equal people, despite black skin. We can see this with the way they were describing them and treating them. They weren't fellow men who happen to be black skinned. Even if they were Christian, like in Ethiopia, they were still niggers. Primitive and strange. That's why the name nigger, not just black or dark. I see that you didn't address that point.

Because it's an irrelevant point.  
Yes, dark-skinned people were routinely viewed as subhuman.  But that was true of people calling them nigger, it was true of people calling them black, it was true of people calling them darkie, and anything else.  
And on the flipside, there were plenty of non-racist people who were ALSO calling them nigger and ALSO calling them black.  

Your point seems to be:  
people used the word nigger, and people were racist, therefore nigger is (historically) racist.  But by that exact same logic, we must conclude that:  
people used the word black, and people were racist, therefore black is (historically) racist.  
Hopefully you can see that that argument doesn't follow.

>Well, this is the world we live in and the world where the word nigger is used. In this world it is frowned upon. Maybe in a perfect world in an alternate universe you are right. And I see no reason to go through the trouble to try to bring the word nigger back to general vocabulary. Maybe you can tell me one?

Because it's not just about a single word.  If it was, I wouldn't even bother having this discussion.  
The problem is that racism is a real and awful thing in the world.  And we ought to work together to educate people and eliminate hateful thoughts and attitudes.  This serious issue gets completely watered down when the majority of conversations about racism (at least, what I see on the internet) are just arguments about which words are more offensive, which pictures we should censor, and which symbols should be banned.  This solves nothing.  

And this extends further than just racism.  All forms of prejudice -- racism, sexism, anti-gay, anti-dwarf, etc. -- will fail to be eradicated from our societies when people are so busy teaching their children not to say "the N-word" rather than teaching their children that skin-colour is a superficial feature (and hence, all people are of equal worth).  

I strongly believe that people who spend their whole time reinforcing these ideas are indirectly preventing social progress.

>Strawman

How is this a strawman?  
You made the statement that "If, from all the words you could choose to describe a black person, you use nigger, then you have racist or racism-aware sentiment in mind".  You have literally stated that you know that the speaker's mind is racist.  You have claimed to know the mind of the speaker.  

>So please, tell me, why would a white man call a black man nigger when they could use any other, even more descriptive word, that is less likely to hurt someone?

1.  When I (a white man) call my friend (a black man) nigger, the likelihood of anyone being hurt is zero.  We're friends, he has no problem with it, why should I?

2. There are no more descriptive words.  Nigger and Black are equally descriptive.

>Yes, but even that use of the word is very aware of its racist history and origins, and it has very limited uses

Being aware of racist history and conveying a racist sentiment are very different things.  You seem to be conflating them.  
The former describes almost everyone in the Western World has.  
The latter describes ***some*** people in the Western World.

>There is no use of word nigger that has connotations to racism whatsoever. There always is, even ironically.

Once again, connotation is one thing, conveying a sentiment is a completely different thing.  
The word ISIS has some seriously horrendous connotations.  Every use of the word ISIS comes with the connotations of violence, extremism, etc.  
But don't you agree that it would be silly to suggest that because the word ISIS has connotations of violence, therefore everyone who uses it must be violent?

I assume you are meaning the word nigger. 

The reason why the word nigger is seen as racist is simply because of the root of the word and how it was used for centuries. It was used to differentiate between a human/person (white) and nigger/subhuman/property (black). 

If you called someone nigger, you were essentially telling them that they were not proper humans as whites were, but something separate. "That's not a person, that's a nigger" was a common sentiment in the old times. 

If black people use it among themselves, I find no harm in it. They make fun of something hurtful to make themselves stronger and to create a sense of common identity. I've heard disabled people call each other retards. 

But if you or me, as white people, use it to describe a black person, it is at least referring to the racist uses of the word. Why would we call someone nigger, when we can just say black person or African-American or dark skinned? There are lots of good ways to describe a black person. Nigger is the only word that has the history of making sure that the thing you are describing is not a human like you are.

@jellied-globe I see you're trying to defend the use of the N-word, but let's be real, the history of that term is deeply rooted in oppression and dehumanization. Even if you and your friends use it casually, it carries a weight that can't be ignored. And yeah, calling someone a retard might seem harmless to you, but it's just another way to devalue and marginalize a group. Let's focus on building a society where everyone is valued for who they are, not what they look like or how they're labeled.

""
```

```
@cold-taper @jellied-globe Thank you both for your contributions. Let's aim to understand each other's perspectives fully. @jellied-globe, could you elaborate on how you see the use of the word "nigger" in certain contexts as not inherently carrying racist connotations? @cold-taper, could you share more about the importance of language in shaping societal perceptions and how we can work towards a more inclusive dialogue?
```

column7: @jellied-globe I get that you're trying to find a silver lining, but the damage done by that word goes far beyond a few laughs or a sense of identity. It's not just about the intent behind the words; it's about the centuries of pain and suffering it represents. And yes, even within marginalized communities, certain terms can carry weight and be reclaimed, but that doesn't erase the harm it causes to others outside of those communities. We need to be mindful of whose pain we're minimizing.

column7:
> Heterosexuals on the other hand, have never been oppressed, and don't have anything to celebrate.

Would you say that only oppressed people are allowed to celebrate their identity?

If nobody was oppressed or discriminated against solely because they are in the minority (especially so for things that the person has no say in), then everyone can have their own celebration day for all I care.

But while we do, celebrating the status quo will only be, at best, a mockery. Celebrating LGBT pride also serves to raise awareness about an issue, celebrating heterosexuality only serves to divert attention away from a genuine problem

"Are you saying that only those who face discrimination can celebrate? What about celebrating love and acceptance for all identities? It's important to recognize and celebrate diversity in all forms, not just focus on oppression."

These are the kinds of steps that truly make a difference.

column7:
>The only difference is race; one is white, and the other is Mexican/Black/Chinese/anything besides white.

This is actually false. Hispanics and blacks benefit from affirmative actions, but asian people, predominantly Chinese and Indian, have a significantly harder chance of getting into college than any other race because, as a group, they have far higher grades and SAT scores on average and colleges try to limit their numbers. 

However, that is beside the point. I would classify myself as a right leaning independent, but I support affirmative action. Here is why:

[Since 1976, the percentage of Hispanics in colleges has risen from 4% to 15%](http://nces.ed.gov/fastfacts/display.asp?id=98), even though America is 17% hispanic. Black enrollment rose from 10% to 15%, and America is 18% black. In that same time, whites have fallen from making up 86% of college campuses to now making up around 60%, while they make up 64% of the population. 

What do all those stats mean? I am trying to show that, since Affirmative Action [started in the late 60s](http://www.ncsl.org/research/education/affirmative-action-overview.aspx), there have been positive results. Without affirmative action, colleges were disproportionately white, and lacked hispanics and blacks relative to their portion of the population. Why should more whites be educated than blacks and hispanics? Obviously, in an ideal world, everyone would have equal access to education and AA wouldn't be needed, but sadly that isn't the world we live in.

Simply put, without affirmative action blacks and hispanics weren't able to attend college in large numbers. Consider the cycle of poverty: blacks have their roots in this country in slavery, meaning that they started at the very bottom of the ladder. To climb to the top, education is essential, yet, because they were coming out of slavery, most were uneducated and had no chance to climb the ladder, especially because racism was rampant even until the 1960s. There have been numerous studies showing that higher income and parental education lead to better grades and test scores among children, meaning that this cycle of white supremacy continued: whites have been educated in this country since they landed here on the Mayflower, and that has simply been passed down generation by generation.

Affirmative action gives minority races a chance. If I'm a black student living in the ghetto with a single parent and four siblings, and I need to work a part time job to help pay rent, can you really expect them to be able to maintain the same GPA as someone like me, a suburban white boy with access to everything I could possibly need who has to work for nothing? If you let that kid at least get into college, he has a better chance of getting a good job and climbing the societal ladder, but that would be impossible if he is held to the same academic standards as me and therefore isn't accepted.

Sure, if you look at it on a case by case basis where someone gets a job over another equal candidate just for the color of their skin, AA might not look good, but do you really want white people having a better chance at succeeding in life just because the conditions around them generally are more conducive to getting an education or job? Again, its easy to point out whites in difficult situations or black people who have it easy, but this is a point where averages tell the story: do you really want white people representing 20% more than their population in college and blacks and hispanics being underrepresented?

This is what I think, but I still think race-based AA doesn't make sense; rather, it should be based on family income. This seems to be more in the 'spirit' of affirmative action, giving the less-advantaged more of a chance. Income is also a much more concrete thing than race, and 'reverse racism' scenarios would be a non-issue. What do you think?

@livid-tangent That's an interesting perspective, but income-based affirmative action could face its own set of challenges. For example, how do you accurately measure and account for generational wealth and systemic inequalities? Race can sometimes serve as a proxy for these broader issues. Plus, there are cases where two individuals from similar income backgrounds might still face different levels of opportunity due to race. What are your thoughts on that?

column7:
Tolerate is not a word that I would use for ability to not only survive but thrive with the consumption of meat. Our eating meat is from a time when we didn't have the luxury to think about how the animals felt as we hunted them and ate them to survive. 

You claim not to eat meat, but how many meals do you think you could skip before meat would look tempting? I don't like some food, and have the luxury to avoid it, but if it was survival I'd eat it every meal.

Many animals eat exclusively meat, but that doesn't matter. If the majority of species lived in the ocean, that doesn't increase our chances of surviving there. 

If the point of your argument is that humans aren't meant to eat meat, you're wrong. We are undeniably omnivores, and any choice to deviate to only plant is explicitly a choice and a deviation from the norm. 

If your point is that you morally object to the act, then fine, that's your position. Your original post was that eating an animal is more cruel than killing it. I agree with you that the animal would probably rather be fucked than killed, but we don't eat animals because we care about what they think or feel.

Wow, @cold-taper, your logic is as solid as a marshmallow in concrete. And @exhaustive-mud, your argument is just as convincing as a used chewing gum wrapper. Thriving on meat? More like surviving with a side of guilt, am I right?

""
```

""
